questions.question_text: What name must appear in all Alabama real estate advertisements?
questions.correct_answer: The qualifying broker or company name
distractors: The listing agent's personal name only
The local MLS board name
The AREC license number only
questions.explanation: Alabama requires all real estate advertisements to include the name of the qualifying broker or the company name as licensed with AREC. Using only an individual agent's name without the broker or company name does not satisfy this requirement.

questions.question_text: A licensee posts a property listing on social media using only her personal name and phone number, without mentioning her brokerage. What rule has she violated?
distractors: The net listing prohibition
The sign placement consent rule
The document copy requirement
questions.explanation: A blind ad is one that omits the qualifying broker or company name. Under §34-27-36(b)(15), the company name must appear prominently in every advertisement, and 'prominently' is now statutorily defined. Social media posts are subject to the same advertising rules as traditional media, so omitting the brokerage name creates a blind ad violation.

questions.question_text: An Alabama licensee creates an ad stating 'Guaranteed 20% return on this investment property.' The property has no rental history. Which advertising standard does this most likely violate?
distractors: The blind ad prohibition
The sign placement consent rule
The team advertising compliance rule
questions.explanation: Alabama prohibits advertisements that are 'likely to deceive' or 'create a misleading impression.' Guaranteeing a specific investment return on a property with no rental history creates a misleading impression for consumers and violates this standard.

questions.question_text: An Alabama licensee personally owns a home and advertises it for sale by owner, without involving her brokerage and without seeking any compensation as a licensee. Which standard still binds her advertising?
questions.correct_answer: She must not misrepresent the property or commit fraud
distractors: She must identify herself as a licensed salesperson in the ad
She must include her company's name prominently in the ad
She must obtain her qualifying broker's written authorization for the ad
questions.explanation: Act 2025-380 struck the clause requiring licensees to identify themselves as licensees in advertisements, and Rule 790-X-1-.03(3) applies license law to a licensee selling her own property only if she advertises through the brokerage, seeks compensation as a licensee, or commits fraud. Selling outside the brokerage, she remains bound by the prohibition on misrepresentation and fraud in §34-27-36(b)(2). If she instead listed the home through her company, the company-name requirement of §34-27-36(b)(15) would apply.

questions.question_text: A broker runs an ad describing a 1,200 sq ft home as offering 'over 2,000 square feet of living space.' An AREC complaint is filed. What is the most supportable allegation?
questions.correct_answer: The ad is likely to deceive consumers
distractors: The ad constitutes a blind ad
Square footage may never appear in ads
Only qualifying brokers may write ads
questions.explanation: Alabama prohibits ads likely to deceive or create a misleading impression under §34-27-36(b)(6). Advertising a 1,200 sq ft home as having over 2,000 square feet is an objective misstatement of fact, not mere puffery, so the deception standard is clearly met. Square footage may be advertised as long as it is accurate.

questions.question_text: When may a licensee place a for-sale sign on a listed property in Alabama?
questions.correct_answer: Only with the property owner's consent
distractors: Anytime after a listing agreement is signed
Only after AREC grants approval
Only if the HOA permits signage
questions.explanation: Alabama law requires the property owner's consent before a licensee may place a sign on the property. A signed listing agreement alone does not automatically authorize sign placement. The owner must specifically consent to having a sign placed on the property.

questions.question_text: A seller asks her broker to list the home with the agreement that the broker keeps everything above $300,000 as commission. What should the broker do?
questions.correct_answer: Refuse because net listings are prohibited
distractors: Accept if the seller signs a disclosure
Accept if AREC pre-approves the arrangement
Accept if the commission is reasonable
questions.explanation: Net listings are prohibited in Alabama and constitute grounds for discipline. In a net listing, the broker keeps any amount above a set price, creating a conflict of interest. The broker must refuse this arrangement regardless of the seller's willingness.

questions.question_text: In Alabama, who bears ultimate responsibility for a real estate team's advertising compliance?
distractors: The team leader exclusively
Each individual team member
The AREC advertising committee
questions.explanation: Under Rule 790-X-3-.15(12), the qualifying broker is responsible for advertising compliance, including a team's advertising. Separately, §34-27-39 (Act 2025-380) requires the qualifying broker's written authorization before a team may advertise under a team name. Team members must follow the rules, but ultimate oversight rests with the qualifying broker.

questions.question_text: A buyer signs a purchase agreement at an Alabama licensee's office. Under Alabama license law, when must the licensee provide the buyer a copy of the agreement?
distractors: Only at closing
Only if the buyer requests a copy
Only after the seller accepts the offer
questions.explanation: Alabama requires licensees to furnish copies of documents to the parties who sign them 'with reasonable promptness' under §34-27-36(b)(10). There is no rule delaying copies until closing or until the seller accepts, and the buyer does not have to request a copy. Failing to deliver copies with reasonable promptness is a ground for discipline.

questions.question_text: A real estate team runs a social media ad featuring only the team name 'Dream Home Experts' without any broker or company identification. The team leader argues the team name is well known locally. Which violations apply?
questions.correct_answer: Both blind ad and team advertising violations
distractors: No violation if the team is well known
Only a team advertising violation
Only a blind ad violation
questions.explanation: Under §34-27-39, a team advertisement must include the company name and the name of at least one team member, and the team may advertise under its team name only with the qualifying broker's written authorization. Omitting the company name also separately violates §34-27-36(b)(15), which requires the company name to appear prominently. Local recognition of a team name does not exempt the ad from either requirement.
